name: FRACTURES OF CERVICAL SPINE

demographics.Prevalence: 4% of cervical spine injuries, 25% of craniocervical injuries

dominant_finding: triangular fragment in soft tissues anterior to vertebral body
discriminating_features: triangular fragment in soft tissues anterior to vertebral body

general: triangular fragment in soft tissues anterior to vertebral body
anterolisthesis of affected vertebra by ½ vertebral body width
widening of posterior portion of disk
disruption of anterior longitudinal ligament, annulus, intervertebral disk, lig. flavum
transverse dimension of anteroinferior avulsion fragment greater than vertical
vertical dimension of triangular fragment greater than transverse dimension
vertically oriented fracture of posterior arch (stable if isolated / part of Jefferson
transverse fracture through inferior pole / midportion at attachment of atlantodental
"bow-tie" sign = the 4 rotated facets on LAT view
rotation of spinous process (on AP view)
loss of posterior vertebral body height with several fragments:
sagittal fracture component extending to inferior endplate
retropulsed fragment from posterior superior margin in spinal canal
comminuted burst fracture of ring of C1 (unstable) with 4 uni- / bilateral
lateral displacement of lateral masses (self-decompressing) on AP view
obliquely oriented fracture through tip of odontoid that is difficult to detect
bilateral vertical pars interarticularis fracture of C2 → separation of body from posterior
fracture through posterior body of C2
prevertebral soft-tissue swelling > 5 mm at anterior-inferior margin of C2
anterior subluxation of C2 on C3:
disruption of C2-C3 posterior vertebral body line
avulsion of anteroinferior corner of C2 (= rupture of anterior longitudinal ligament) =

5-8% of patients with fractures may have normal radiographs!
clinical.Cx: neurologic deterioration with delay in diagnosis
clinical.Prognosis: bone fusion in almost 100% with collar / halo immobilization
